3: The food and beverages sector is the largest manufacturing sector in Europe in terms of revenue and employment (Food Drink Europe, 2011), with significant environmental side effects (Garnett, 2009; Iribarren et al., 2011; Fantin et al., 2012). In this context, the dairy sector must receive special attention for several reasons: i) within the European food and beverages sector, the dairy division holds the top position in terms of innovative markets (Ramirez et al., 2006; Berlin and Sonesson, 2008; Euromilk, 2012); ii) milk and dairy products are important ingredients in the human diet (Bachmann, 2001; Munoz et al., 2010); and iii) the high demand of dairy products has fostered changes in production practices

towards the production of organic milk in order to reduce the environmental impact in terms of eutrophying and toxicity emis- sions as well as energy use (de Boer, 2003; Thomassen et al., 2008; de Vries and de Boer, 2010) and the application of the best man- agement practices (BIDF, 2009; del Prado et al., 2010). The dairy sector involves the production of several products, manufactured in very different production lines, but always origi- nating from the same raw material: raw milk. Within dairy prod- ucts, nowadays cheese is experiencing an increasing demand and is the most consumed dairy product after drinking milk (Rohner- Thielen, 2008). Moreover, over 36% of the total whole milk pro- duced in Europe in 2010 was processed into cheese, while only 12.4% was used for drinking milk (European Commission, 2012). According to the Eurostat statistics (2011), Spain occupies the seventh position in Europe in terms of production volume of dairy products (ca. 5.8 million tonnes in 2011). Spanish dairy farms are mainly located in ten regions, accounting for 92% of the total Spanish cows. One of these regions is Galicia (NW Spain), leader in the

production of raw milk: 37.7% of the Spanish production and ca. 2 million tonnes per year (FEGA, 2012; Xunta de Galicia, 2012). Agri- cultural activity in Galicia is depicted by a wide diversity of land use, production models, farm sizes and geographical dispersion (Álvarez-López et al., 2008). However, the modernization activities developed in the farms in recent years highlight the role of the Galician dairy sector in a framework of quality and innovation (Xunta de Galicia, 2012).

Regarding the consumption of cheese in Spain, it represents 3.1% of the total food expenses per family and the average annual con- sumption per capita is ca. 6.2 kg (Martín Cerdeño, 2008). There are currently 26 Protected Designation of Origin (PDO) in Spain regu- lated by the Spanish Ministry of Agriculture, according to climatic, geographical and cultural variations. Among them, cow milk is the raw material used for the elaboration of twelve different cheeses (Cerespain, 2012) and Galicia is the leader in the manufacture of cow milk cheeses. Therefore, this study focusses on the production of one single Galician cheese derived from cow milk and labelled with a PDO: San Simon da Costa cheese. The quantification of the environmental effects derived from its manufacture is the main goal of this study since this type of cheese is produced following a traditional recipe, although with an exhaustive state of the art sanitary control. To do so, the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) meth- odology (ISO 14040, 2006) was considered in this study.

To date, this environmental methodology has just been applied for the evaluation of the environmental performance of different milk production systems at farms and dairy mills (such as Castanheira et al., 2010; Iribarren et al., 2011; Hospido et al., 2003; Bartl et al., 2011; Fantin et al., 2012). In addition, the production of other dairy products such as semi-hard cheese (Berlin, 2002; González-García et al., Submitted for publication; van Middelaar et al., 2011), mozzarella cheese (Nielsen and Hoier, 2009), milk powder (Ramirez et al., 2006) or butter and blend products (Flysjö, 2011) have also been assessed using LCA. An example of an available database where the dairy sector has been assessed in detail is the Danish LCA Food database (Nielsen et al., 2003). However, there is no environmental information about the production of Spanish cheese and, therefore, this paper, focused on the production of San Simon cheese, can be of interest not only in the Spanish context, but also for the European dairy sector since to the best of our knowledge, it presents for the first time a detailed report of the life cycle inventory and environmental assessment of a cheese production in Spain.

8: The goal of this study was to identify and quantify the envi- ronmental impacts derived from the manufacture of one of the most popular and traditional Galician cheeses: San Simon da Costa cheese (Regulation (EC) No 1229/2008, 2008). Moreover, this study aims not only at quantifying the environmental profile but also at identifying the most critical steps in the production line of a dairy mill considered representative of the San Simon da Costa cheese sector.
9: The functional unit considered was 1 kg of cheese packaged and ready to be consumed, which corresponds to the average weight of the large format, i.e. the most common option available in Spanish markets. The selection of this unit is in agreement with other cheese studies (Berlin, 2002; Nielsen and Hoier, 2009). The mois- ture and fat content of this type of cheese is around 42% and 45%, respectively (As Fontelas, 2012).
Fig. 1. Geographical location of the San Simon da Costa protected designation of origin cheeses production area - Terra Cha (Lugo, Galicia).
10: San Simon da Costa PDO cheeses are produced in the Terra Cha region (Fig. 1), which has a long tradition of cheese making. The local dairy herds are mainly composed of Galician Blonde, Brown Swiss and Friesian breeds, which provide the high quality raw material for the San Simon da Costa cheese, since only milk pro- duced in this area can be used for its production.

Commonly, there are two cheese formats. The large format - matured for at least 45 days - weighing 0.8-1.5 kg (13-18 cm in height) and the small one - matured for at least 30 days - weighing 0.4-0.8 kg (10-13 cm in height). In both formats, the cheese shape is characteristic with the upper part being pointed (Fig. The rind is smoked, rigid (1-3 mm thickness) and presents an ochre-yellow colour. The body is of fine texture, fatty and with a characteristic flavour and aroma. This type of cheese can be included within the group of semi-hard cheeses.
11: <header>2.4. Description of the system under study </header>

The production of San Simon da Costa cheese follows a tradi- tional recipe, where two elements provide the cheese with its particular flavour and aroma. Firstly, the basic raw material is the milk produced by the cows bred in the local area under the highest standards of hygiene and quality and, secondly, the smoking pro- cess using Galician birch wood exclusively.

Fig. 3. System boundaries and flow chart of the cheese production life cycle. Dark gray subsystem boxes are based on collected real data. Light gray subsystem box is based on database.

The manufacture process described below is representative of the dairy mills and follows the standardized and certified protocol to obtain the PDO (Regulation (EC) No 1229/2008, 2008). The production line was divided in three subsystems: dairy farm, dairy factory and wastewater treatment (Fig. 3). In addition, dairy factory activities were divided in four main stages: i) treat- ment, ii) coagulation and moulding, iii) salting and smoking and finally, iv) packaging.

Subsystem 1 - Dairy farm: The main raw material for the production of cheese is the raw milk produced in the Terra Cha region. Around 17% of the processed milk is produced in the farm located at the premises and the remaining milk is sup- plied from four nearby dairy farms. No major differences were observed between the farms considered. The San Simon da Costa PDO regulation demands the use of milk from three specific species of cows and from local farms. Thus, a typical farm located in the Terra Cha region was considered for the collection of the inventory data. Fig. 3. System boundaries and flow chart of the cheese production life cycle. Dark gray subsystem boxes are based on collected real data. Light gray subsystem box is based on database.

The manufacture process described below is representative of the dairy mills and follows the standardized and certified protocol to obtain the PDO (Regulation (EC) No 1229/2008, 2008). The production line was divided in three subsystems: dairy farm, dairy factory and wastewater treatment (Fig. 3). In addition, dairy factory activities were divided in four main stages: i) treat- ment, ii) coagulation and moulding, iii) salting and smoking and finally, iv) packaging.

Subsystem 1 - Dairy farm: The main raw material for the production of cheese is the raw milk produced in the Terra Cha region. Around 17% of the processed milk is produced in the farm located at the premises and the remaining milk is sup- plied from four nearby dairy farms. No major differences were observed between the farms considered. The San Simon da Costa PDO regulation demands the use of milk from three specific species of cows and from local farms. Thus, a typical farm located in the Terra Cha region was considered for the collection of the inventory data. The values were validated with a broader study of Galician dairy farms (Iribarren et al., 2011). This subsystem included all the agricultural activities related with the production of the animal feed (i.e. maize silage, grass

<list>silage, straw, hay, fodder and alfalfa) and other inputs such as diesel (used by tractors in food distribution and maintenance of stables), electricity (milking and milk storage systems, lighting and stables maintenance), lubricating oil and cleaning agents. Diffuse emissions to air derived from livestock (CH4 and N20) and from manure storage (NH3) were also considered (Hospido et al., 2003; Castanheira et al., 2010). 
Subsystem 2 - Dairy factory: this subsystem includes all the ac- tivities that take place in the cheese making mill, from the milk reception to cheese packaging. The raw milk 1 received in the dairy mill is maintained in refrigerated tanks for the subsequent activities. It is important to remark that the use of powder milk or butter is not permitted in the San Simon cheese PDO.</list>

The first step in the cheese manufacture system is the thermal treatment or pasteurisation, where the milk is heated up to 72 by means of a boiler fed with fuel oil. Thereafter, the pasteurized milk is sent to the fermentation tank where several additives are added: rennet, calcium chloride and bacterial cultures. Rennet triggers the
1 1 kg of milk is equal to 1.05 kg ECM (Energy Corrected Milk).

coagulation of the milk to obtain the curd in a process controlled by a coagulum; the addition of calcium chloride provides a firmer gel. The addition of casein or other related derivatives is not performed. The fermentation process takes 30-40 min at 31-33 °C. The co- agulum is then cut into small pieces with wires. Stirring is started once the pieces shrink slightly and partially expel the whey. Thereafter, prolonged stirring and heating of the pieces facilitate the drainage of the whey and the curd is finally formed. The pro- duction processes of the additives were considered out of the sys- tem boundaries due to the small amount required and the limited information concerning their production. This assumption was supported by Berlin (2002), where the authors reported that a negligible contribution to the whole environmental profile can be attributed to the production processes of additives.

Once the curd is formed, it is sent to the pressing and moulding step. To start with, a fraction of the whey is separated in a metal mesh and the curd is cut, put into moulds with its characteristic shape (Fig. 2) and pressed (for 10-12 h) to remove the remaining whey content. Whey represents about 80-90% of the volume of milk used in the cheese production line (Nadais et al., 2010) and whey composition accounts for more than 50% of the milk solids including 20% of proteins and most of the lactose (Nadais et al., 2010). Due to its characteristics, whey has multiple applications on feed and food industries. However, in small-scale cheese pro- duction facilities (as the ones of San Simon cheese mills), whey is not usually transformed on-site due to the high investment costs related to recovery technologies (Malaspina et al., 1995; Nadais et al., 2010). So, whey is here mixed with the wastewater effluent from the mill and the final stream is sent to a large tank as a storage step previous to the final treatment outside the facilities.

Once the curd has been pressed, it is sent to the salting process where it is mixed with brine (concentration of 14-17%) in tanks for a maximum of 24 h. Thereafter, the cheese is ripened in a room for 45 days (large format) or 30 days (small format) at 8 °C. Finally, it is smoked in order to obtain its characteristic colour and flavour by the burning of birch wood supplied from local forests. Finally, it is pack- aged for distribution up to the retailers in cardboard boxes for one, two or four units.

Subsystem 3 - Wastewater Treatment: dairy mills produce large amounts of wastewater with a high organic load (Nadais et al., 2010; Bartl et al., 2011; Fantin et al., 2012). As previously stated, the dairy factory does not have its own wastewater treatment. Every three days, 8000 L of wastewater are collected in a tanker and sent by road to the nearby municipal WWTP for further treatment (ca. 28.6 km), which comprises three stages (primary, secondary and tertiary treatments) before realising the treated water into the water course and applying the generated sludge on agricultural land. Both the transport and the waste treatment have also been included in the analysis.
Production of capital goods (machinery and buildings) was excluded from the study in accordance with Berlin (2002), Hogaas Eide (2002) and Iribarren et al. (2011).
7: In the production system under study, there are two different subsystems which are multi-output. In the dairy farm, the outputs are milk, meat, skin (for leather) and manure. An economic allo- cation approach was considered for milk and meat, which is the most common approach used (de Vries and de Boer, 2010). Con- cerning manure, its use as organic fertilizer in the agricultural processes for the production of the different feed inputs was considered and, therefore, the associated on-field emissions were
taken into account (data not shown on Table 1). The skin was excluded from the assessment due to its much lower importance as reported by Hogaas Eide (2002) and Hospido et al. (2003).

Economic allocation factors of 92% (milk) and 8% (meat) were obtained from the current economic turnovers for both products (Xunta de Galicia, 2012). As performed by Iribarren et al. (2011), the material flows exclusively quantified for dairy COWS were entirely allocated to milk, such as feed inputs and direct emissions from livestock. The remaining flows such as cleaning agents, electricity and fossil fuels were distributed between both co-products following economic allocation.

Cheese making factories are also multi-output systems, where liquid whey is produced together with cheese. In large dairy mills, whey is typically converted into whey powder and sent to other in- dustries for further applications (e.g. feed and food products). How- ever, San Simon cheese mills have yet to acquire the equipment required for whey conversion. Hence, the amount produced (=9L whey kg cheese) is not treated on-site but discharged with the wastewater for further treatment in the municipal WWTP. Cheese is the only product obtained and, therefore, the environmental bur- dens associated with the whole process were totally allocated to it.

Cha region was assessed and inventoried in detail in order to gather the life cycle inventory (LCI) data for the subsystem where the raw milk is produced (Table 1). Background information (such as water and silage plastic requirements) was taken from Iribarren et al. (2011). Although raw milk is supplied from five dairy farms, data reported here arrived from one of them since no large differences between them were identified, mainly due to the geographical proximity and characteristics (same type of herds, similar number of cows per herd and climatic conditions).

Concerning the animal feed, the LCI related with the production of maize silage, grass silage and straw was taken from Castanheira et al. (2010), fodder production from Hospido et al. (2003), alfalfa from Gallego et al. (2011), and hay production from the Ecoinvent database (Nemecek and Käggi, 2007). Regarding the manure pro- duced by the dairy farm, it is reused within the farm as organic fertilizer and it has been included in the agricultural production processes of ryegrass and maize.

Electricity generation (Spanish profile), diesel production (and related combustion emissions in the agricultural machineries), lubricating oil and cleaning agents manufacture were also taken from the Ecoinvent database (Althaus et al., 2007; Jungbluth, 2007).

The foreground inventory data for the cheese making dairy factory (Subsystem 2) consisted of average annual data for year 2011 obtained by on-site measurements in the dairy mill. 15: Among the steps defined within the life cycle impact assess- ment stage of the standardized LCA methodology, only the classi- fication and characterization stages were undertaken here (ISO 14040, 2006). The characterization factors reported by the Centre of Environmental Science of Leiden University (CML 2001 method) were used (Guinée et al., 2001). The following impact categories were evaluated according to the CML method v2.04: abiotic depletion potential (ADP), acidification potential (AP), eutrophica- tion potential (EP), global warming potential (GWP), ozone layer depletion potential (ODP) and photochemical oxidants formation potential (POFP). Furthermore, an energy analysis was carried out based on the cumulative non-renewable fossil and nuclear energy demand (CED) computed according to Hischier et al. (2009) as an additional indicator.

Concerning biogenic CO2, emissions from birch wood combus- tion in the smoking process and uptake during the biomass growth by photosynthesis have been quantified. However, they have not computed to the GWP since a characterisation factor of zero has been considered for biogenic CO2.

The software SimaPro 7.3.2 was used for the computational implementation of the inventories (PRé Consultants, 2012). Land use and soil quality changes caused by the agricultural activities were excluded from the assessment due to the lack of valuable information and data.
The characterisation environmental results for the San Simon cheese production process are displayed in Table 4 for each impact category as well as for the flow indicator (CED).

According to the results, it is clear that the production of the raw milk at the dairy farm presented a significant contribution to the environmental impact in levels ranging from 63% (ADP and CED) to 89% (AP and EP). 20: Fig. 5 displays the contributions from the different processes involved in the dairy farm for the production of the raw milk. Contributions derived from this subsystem are dominated by two processes: the production of animal feed and the on-farm emis- sions derived from farm activities.

On-farm emissions involved methane emissions from enteric fermentation and faeces and urine; and N20 and NH3 emissions from manure storage and water emissions (COD and TSS) derived from cleaning activities. NH3 emissions were responsible for 79% and 24% of AP and EP, respectively. COD emissions contributed to 3% of EP. CH4 emissions accounted for 39% and 57% of GWP and POFP, respectively. N20 emissions added up to 5% of GWP. Thus, faeces and urine, enteric fermentation and manure storage are remarkable environmental hot spots, which is in agreement with other re- ported studies (Berlin, 2002; Bartl et al., 2011; Fantin et al., 2012).

Concerning the production of animal feed, its contribution to the environmental profile ranged from 34% to 86% depending on the categories. The feed for the cows is composed by ryegrass silage (36%), fodder (31%), maize silage (18%), straw (8%), hay (4%) and alfalfa (3%). All the agricultural activities (including the machinery, diesel and fertilizers used there), diffuse derived emissions from

25: <header>3.1.2. Environmental profile excluding the dairy farm (Subsystems 2 and 3) </header>

The contributions to different impact categories excluding the production of raw milk (Subsystem 1) are shown in Fig. 7. According to these results, there are several processes with remarkable con- tributions to the environmental profile. The activities related with the treatment of the wastewater in the municipal WWTP are an important environmental hot spot, especially in terms of EP (78%), mainly due to the direct discharge of phosphate (33%), nitrate (19%), ammonium (15%) and COD (7%) to the water bed. The delivery of the wastewater stream from the dairy factory to the WWTP (ca. 28.6 km by tanker) presented considerable contributions, espe- cially for AP (7%), ADP (8%), CED (8%) and ODP (13%). Diesel re- quirements and combustion derived emissions are the main carriers of these environmental results.

\As previously mentioned, one of the main characteristics of this type of cheese is the smoking step with birch wood from local for- ests. The production of birch wood and the combustion of the wood in the boiler were taken into account in this subsystem (Fig. 7). The contributions from both sources added up to 21% in the GWP cate- gory, 41% for AP and 53% for POFP. These important contributions were mainly due to combustion emissions from the use of diesel in forest machinery, as well as from wood combustion in the boiler. It is important to remark that the contribution from this process to the POFP due to carbon monoxide emissions (13%) and sulphur dioxide (22%) emissions derived from the combustion steps.

The cheese dairy mill requires significant electricity use to operate the machinery, refrigeration, lighting and ventilation. The electricity production required to power the dairy factory is taken directly from the Spanish grid and it is another environmental hot spot in all the categories examined, with contributions ranging from 20% (GWP) to 29% (AP). The Spanish electricity profile highly depends on fossil fuels. Thus, carbon dioxide, sulphur dioxide and halon 1211 emissions dominated these contributions.

Dairy factories (i.e. cheese production process) present signifi- cant energy consumption (Ramirez et al., 2006). Large amounts of energy are consumed as thermal energy obtained from the com- bustion of fossil fuels (i.e., fuel oil) to produce steam and hot water for heating and cleaning operations (mainly in the pasteurisation step). The production of fuel oil and its combustion in the boiler are remarkable contributors to categories such as GWP (25%), CED (26%), ADP (27%) and ODP (39%). This process is responsible for 21%

Fig. 7. Results (in %) for each impact category and activity involved in the cheese making factory (Subsystem 2) and the wastewater treatment (Subsystem 3). Impact category ac- ronyms: =Abiotic Depletion Potential, Acidification Potential, Eutrophication Potential, GWP = Global Warming Potential, ODP = Ozone Layer Depletion Potential, POFP = Photo-Oxidants Formation Potential and CED = Cumulative Energy Demand.
of carbon dioxide emissions and 39% of halon 1301 emissions derived from fuel oil combustion and extraction, respectively.

Finally, regarding the inputs supply, it is a hot spot in terms of GWP (12%), CED (13%), ADP (13%) and ODP (20%). The main responsible of these results is the distribution of 83% of the raw milk from the nearby farms (total distance of the milk collection circuit is ca. 200 km).

29: <header>3.2. Comparison of the environmental results with other related studies </header>

The production of raw milk, the main raw material in the cheese production line, is clearly the process which contributed most to the environmental impact in all the categories under assessment (Fig. 4). This high contribution to the environmental profile is in agreement with other related studies where not only cheese (e.g. semi-hard cheese) but also other dairy products (e.g. pasteurised or powdered milk and yogurt) were assessed from an environmental point of view (Berlin, 2002; Berlin and Sonesson, 2008; Hogaas Eide, 2002; Iribarren et al., 2011; Ramirez et al., 2006; Fantin et al., 2012).

A common result in all these studies is the remarkable contri- bution of the dairy farm in categories such as AP, EP, GWP and POFP. This is mainly due to methane emissions derived from the enteric fermentation process (GWP and POFP), as well as to ammonia emissions related to manure management and its application to soil (AP and EP). Although these contributions have been also re- ported by other authors (; Berlin, 2002; Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003; Castanheira et al., 2010; Bartl et al., 2011; Yan et al., 2011), there are several differences which depend considerably on the emissions factors (g per dairy cow) assumed for the methane and ammonia from enteric fermentation and faeces, urine and manure storage (Bartl et al., 2011), roughage consumption as well as on the farm regime (organic VS conventional, intensive VS extensive, Thus, some authors (Thomassen et al., 2008; Van der Werf et al., 2009) reported that organic milk production implies higher methane emissions than the conventional process due to the lower milk production level per cow and the increased use of roughage. Both the high intake of roughage and the low content of starch in the roughage in the animal feed imply lower fermentation rapidity in the rumen (Thomassen et al., 2008). Therefore, methane emissions ranged from 109 to 155 kg methane per cow and year (Castanheira et al., 2010; Haas et al., 2001). In our study, we considered 113.2 kg methane per cow and year which fits within the range proposed by Thomassen et al. (2008) for conventional dairy farms. The same problem occurs with other emissions such as ammonia from manure in the stable, from the inside and outside manure storage and from manure application which is also calcu- lated by means of emission factors.

The dairy farm activities (e.g. lighting, refrigeration, milking step and pumping to the tank lorry) require electricity. Other ancillary activities require diesel for their machineries. Thus, an important amount of energy is required in the dairy farm, which also con- tributes to these categories by means of combustion emissions as well as to ADP and CED. In this study, this electricity represented 7% of the CED in the dairy farm. This value is considerably lower than the one reported by Hogaas Eide (2002), which was 13% of the total. This difference could be based on the characteristics of the dairy farm (different production scale) and automation degree. Annual milk production per dairy farm and milk production per cow is considerably different between both scenarios, being lower in our case study (Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003). According to Hogaas Eide (2002), small production scale is not always more environmental friendly than large-scale, especially when dealing with dairy farms, due to higher energy requirements (the equip- ment has to be cleaned and started up regardless the volume).

Special attention must also be paid to the animal feed produc- tion. In this study, and according to Fig. 5, this factor is the main hot spot of the contributions from the dairy farm (Subsystem 1) in all the categories (ca. ratios higher than 50%), except for POFP where it is placed in second position (32%). As shown in Fig. 6, fodder pro- duction is the main responsible of the environmental load from animal feed production with contributions of up to 82%. This dominance has been also reported by other authors, i.e. Hogaas Eide (2002) and Hospido et al. (2003). In addition, this factor is also one of the key contributors to ammonia emissions and leakage of nutrients with remarkable contributions to AP and EP.

Concerning the remaining subsystems (2 and 3), two processes must receive special attention since they are responsible for more than 40% of the contributions to all the categories except to EP: the cheese smoking step and the heating production system. Both processes are combustion systems where birch wood and fuel oil are used for the cheese smoking and the production of thermal energy. Combustion emissions derived from both were the prin- cipal responsible of these results in categories such as AP, ODP and POFP. The smoking step is not common to other cheese production lines assessed in the literature and therefore, it was not possible to compare our results with other studies. However, the production of thermal requirements from fossil fuels was identified as an important environmental factor in other studies in categories such as GWP, AP, EP and POFP (Berlin., 2002; Hospido et al., 2003).

34: Concerning the wastewater effluent treatment, it is common that small scale cheese mills have no specific equipment for whey processing (Nadais et al., 2010) and, thus, whey stream is sent together with the wastewater to the WWTP. Hence, around half of the San Simon cheese production factories send the whey stream to the WWTP, while the other half delivers the whey stream for further processing. For the latter, the whey stream is sent to a dairy factory located not far away (ca. the distribution radio is 70 km) where whey is converted into whey powder. This alternative for whey should improve the profile of this cheese from an environ- mental point of view due to the valorisation of the whey since the generation of wastewater is reduced and a valuable product is obtained. Moreover, whey valorisation should involve the produc- tion of a food co-product with good calorific value, which could justify the resources needed.

Thus, this potential alternative was proposed in order to deter- mine the associated environmental improvement. This alternative scenario should include the following subsystems: dairy farm (Subsystem 1), cheese making dairy factory (Subsystem 2a), WWTP (Subsystem 3a) and whey processing dairy mill (Subsystem 4). Subsystem 2a corresponds to the cheese making factory under assessment (Subsystem 2) with a number of modifications. Therefore, this subsystem has two separate output streams: San Simon cheese and liquid whey. The environmental loads from common processes (e.g. raw milk production) should be allocated between the co-products taking into account an allocation approach based on the fat content. This allocation method had only been proposed by Teulon (1993), and it was assumed in this particular case study for two reasons: a) the whey stream has no economic value for this type of cheese making factories and b) Teulon (1993), who also assumed economic allocation, obtained similar results to those presented in this study (Berlin, 2002). Hence, an average fat content of 0.36% and 45% for the liquid whey and the San Simon cheese respectively was considered (As Fontelas, 2012) and, as a result, the allocation factors obtained were 7% and 93% respectively. All the processes of the dairy mill were allocated according to these factors, with the exception of the packaging

materials production, the smoking step and the wastewater treat- ment (now subsystem 3a: 4.4 L kg of cheese with an average composition of 5.3 COD (European Commission, 2006)), all of which were totally assigned to the cheese.

The whey stream (ca. L per kg of cheese) is delivered by trucks to a nearby dairy mill (Subsystem 4) where it should be processed into whey powder. It was not possible to identify the inventory data for this step due to confidentiality issues. However, the energy re- quirements in the dairy mill for the whey conversion were taken into account according to data from the literature. Liquid whey conver- sion into powder is a high energy intensive process which requires electricity and thermal energy: 4.61-6.64 kWh/kg of liquid whey processed, from which 18% corresponds to electricity and 82% to thermal energy requirements (Dairy Sustainability Website, 2012). Thus, a requirement of 1.20 kWh of electricity and 5.44 kWh of thermal energy per kg of whey powder obtained were assumed. Electricity requirements were taken directly from the Spanish grid and thermal energy is produced on-site from fuel oil combustion in boilers. Combustion emissions derived from the boilers were included within this subsystem boundary. Other derived water emissions (e.g. COD) and waste from the whey processing were not included due to the lack of data. Therefore, the flows corresponding to Subsystem 4 were exclusively attributed to the whey powder production.

The total environmental results obtained for this alternative scenario are displayed in Table 5 where the loads are distributed to the cheese and whey powder production. The total loads for the whole scenario are slightly higher than the base scenario in all the categories except for EP, where a reduction of 7% in the equivalent phosphate emissions is achieved. In the remaining categories, the contributions to each impact category range from 4% in AP to 8% in ADP and ODP. The reduction in EP is associated with the recovery of the whey stream and its further conversion into whey powder, which result into lower water emissions. The increased values in the remaining categories are considerably influenced by the whey processing stage which is an energy intensive process (evaporation, crystallization and spray drying steps) and demands high elec- tricity and heat inputs.

It is interesting to remark that in the alternative scenario, the environmental loads are distributed into two value-added products, cheese and whey powder. Thus, if the environmental loads corre- sponding to cheese are assessed in detail (Table 5), they are consid- erably reduced in comparison with the base scenario. Reductions ranging from 7% in POFP to 15% in EP are achieved with the recovery of the whey stream. Fig. 8a and b display the ratios of the responsible subsystems to the impact categories under study corresponding to cheese and whey powder. According to these figures, Subsystem 1, which involves the dairy farm related activities, is again the main carrier of environmental impacts in all the categories. Moreover, the

Hence, around half of the San Simon cheese production factories send the whey stream to the WWTP, while the other half delivers the whey stream for further processing. This alternative scenario should include the following subsystems: dairy farm (Subsystem 1), cheese making dairy factory (Subsystem 2a), WWTP (Subsystem 3a) and whey processing dairy mill (Subsystem 4). Subsystem 2a corresponds to the cheese making factory under assessment (Subsystem 2) with a number of modifications. Therefore, this subsystem has two separate output streams: San Simon cheese and liquid whey. The environmental loads from common processes (e.g. raw milk production) should be allocated between the co-products taking into account an allocation approach based on the fat content. This allocation method had only been proposed by Teulon (1993), and it was assumed in this particular case study for two reasons: a) the whey stream has no economic value for this type of cheese making factories and b) Teulon (1993), who also assumed economic allocation, obtained similar results to those presented in this study (Berlin, 2002). Hence, an average fat content of 0.36% and 45% for the liquid whey and the San Simon cheese respectively was considered (As Fontelas, 2012) and, as a result, the allocation factors obtained were 7% and 93% respectively. All the processes of the dairy mill were allocated according to these factors, with the exception of the packaging

materials production, the smoking step and the wastewater treat- ment (now subsystem 3a: 4.4 L kg of cheese with an average composition of 5.3 COD (European Commission, 2006)), all of which were totally assigned to the cheese.

The whey stream (ca. L per kg of cheese) is delivered by trucks to a nearby dairy mill (Subsystem 4) where it should be processed into whey powder. It was not possible to identify the inventory data for this step due to confidentiality issues. However, the energy re- quirements in the dairy mill for the whey conversion were taken into account according to data from the literature. Liquid whey conver- sion into powder is a high energy intensive process which requires electricity and thermal energy: 4.61-6.64 kWh/kg of liquid whey processed, from which 18% corresponds to electricity and 82% to thermal energy requirements (Dairy Sustainability Website, 2012). Thus, a requirement of 1.20 kWh of electricity and 5.44 kWh of thermal energy per kg of whey powder obtained were assumed. Electricity requirements were taken directly from the Spanish grid and thermal energy is produced on-site from fuel oil combustion in boilers. Combustion emissions derived from the boilers were included within this subsystem boundary. Other derived water emissions (e.g. COD) and waste from the whey processing were not included due to the lack of data. Therefore, the flows corresponding to Subsystem 4 were exclusively attributed to the whey powder production.

42: Cheese is the second most consumed dairy product in Spain and an important item in the Spanish food basket. Although there are multiple types of cheeses, only 26 types are labelled with a Pro- tected Designation of Origin (PDO) label, which provides recogni- tion to the quality of the cheese. In this study, the production of a semi-hard PDO cheese - San Simon da Costa, one of the most characteristic Galician cheeses has been assessed. San Simon da Costa cheese is a very popular diary product with a growing market not only in Galicia and Spain but also abroad. Its smoking step with
birch wood (a typical tree cultivated on the surroundings) is a distinctive characteristic. Moreover, this is the unique Spanish smoked cheese made exclusively from raw cow milk.

A Galician dairy farm and a cheese making factory were assessed in detail from a cradle to gate perspective by means of a detailed collection of inventory data. The main conclusion extracted from this study is that the farm activities are the most relevant source of environmental impacts in all the categories under assessment with contributions ranging from 63% to 89%. Feed production (mainly fodder), together with on-farm emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management, were the main carriers of these contributions.

Cheese making activities were also deemed important and special attention must be paid to three steps: the smoking process, the heating system and the wastewater treatment. As this dairy factory lacks a whey recovery system because of its small-scale production, the whey stream mixed with the wastewater effluent is sent to the municipal WWTP which involves important eutro- phying emissions.

The possibility of sending the whey stream to a nearby dairy factory for its conversion into whey powder was considered as an improvement action by the cheese producer. According to the re- sults, it is possible to achieve important reductions in the envi- ronmental profile of the PDO San Simon cheese in categories such as eutrophication and ozone layer depletion; however, attention must be paid to the environmental loads assigned to the whey powder, especially regarding those derived from electricity and heating requirements.

Therefore, this study shows the environmental impacts associ- ated with one of the most important dairy products present in the human diet: cheese. The use of primary data guarantees the quality and relevance of the results obtained. The definition and imple- mentation of actions by the cheese producer in order to guide its continuous improvement efforts could be used for other food in- dustries as a reference to improve their production systems. This environmental assessment is thought to be valid for other types of cheeses since remarkable similarities have been identified between the production of this smoked cheese and other types of cheeses (semi-hard or mature), produced under different processes and in different countries.
